strapline: The oldest professional derby in world football. Just 300 yards and the River Trent separate them. Notts County, founded in 1862, are the oldest professional club in the world — and they even gave Juventus their famous black and white stripes.

Separated by barely a mile along the Thames. Fulham are actually the older club, founded in 1879, but Chelsea were specifically created in 1905 to fill Stamford Bridge — which was originally built as an athletics venue.

When West Ham moved to the Olympic Stadium in 2016, Orient chairman Barry Hearn led the legal challenge. Orient's Brisbane Road is closer to the Olympic Park than Upton Park ever was. Orient argued it would destroy their fanbase. The move went ahead anyway.

The "Friendly Derby" — though try telling that to fans. Everton were the original Anfield tenants until a rent dispute with the landlord John Houlding in 1892 led them to move across Stanley Park. Houlding kept the ground and founded Liverpool FC. Families in this city are literally split down the middle.

The Potteries Derby. Both from different parts of Stoke-on-Trent, a federation of six towns. When Port Vale were expelled from the Football League in 1907 for financial irregularities, it took them until 1919 to get back. Stoke, meanwhile, were founding members of the Football League itself.

Separated only by the Tyne. Gateshead once played in the Football League (1930-1960) but were controversially voted out and replaced by Peterborough. The original Gateshead AFC folded in 1977, but the phoenix club has risen back through the pyramid.

North London non-league neighbours. Wealdstone famously won the Conference and FA Trophy double in 1985 — a feat that's never been repeated. They then fell through financial ruin and spent years groundsharing at various venues, including at one point sharing with their rivals Barnet.

The Second City Derby and one of the most hostile in English football. The clubs are just over two miles apart. The rivalry intensified during the 2010 League Cup quarter-final when a Birmingham fan ran onto the pitch and attacked Villa's Jack Grealish — who got up and scored the winner.

Kent neighbours separated by two and a half miles. Ebbsfleet made worldwide headlines in 2008 when they were bought by the crowdfunding website MyFootballClub, giving 30,000 online members voting rights on team affairs. It didn't last.

Hampshire neighbours with a phoenix club twist. The original Aldershot FC went bust in 1992 mid-season — the first Football League club to fold since Accrington Stanley in 1962. The current Aldershot Town rose from the ashes.
Just three miles apart in the West Midlands. Rushall Olympic play at Dales Lane, a ground with a capacity under 2,000 — roughly 1/5th the size of Walsall's Bescot Stadium next door.

The Steel City Derby. Wednesday were originally a cricket club whose members played football on Wednesdays (their half-day off). United were formed by the Sheffield United Cricket Club. Both emerged from the sporting culture of the same steel city. The clubs have shared the same division for less than half of their combined history, making each meeting rare and highly charged.

The Bristol Derby. Rovers have been nomads — they've played at more grounds than almost any English club, and were groundless for years, playing at Bath City's Twerton Park from 1986 to 1996. City stayed put at Ashton Gate, lording it over their neighbours.
Just five miles apart in Hampshire. Eastleigh's rise through non-league has been funded by chairman Stewart Donald, who later bought Sunderland in 2018 — not the most popular tenure on Wearside.

Both from the south bank of the Tyne, six miles apart. South Shields were founded in 1888 and have experienced multiple reformations. Their current iteration has been on a rapid rise through the non-league pyramid.

West London neighbours just two and a half miles apart. Both have had periods as fashionable London clubs — QPR had their Rodney Marsh and Stan Bowles era in the 70s, while Fulham attracted Johnny Haynes and later became Mohamed Al-Fayed's vanity project, complete with a Michael Jackson statue outside Craven Cottage.

The oldest rivalry in West London. Brentford's move to the Gtech Community Stadium in 2020 actually brought them slightly closer to Fulham's Craven Cottage. In the 2019/20 season, Brentford's "Moneyball" approach under Matthew Benham transformed them from a yo-yo club into a Premier League side.
County Durham neighbours, eight miles apart. Spennymoor play at the wonderfully named Brewery Field — named because it sits next to the former Whitbread brewery site.

South-east London's simmering grudge match. Charlton's return to The Valley in 1992 after seven years of exile (they'd been groundsharing at Selhurst Park and Upton Park) was an emotional homecoming. Having Millwall just down the road added edge to every fixture.

The North London Derby. Arsenal were originally from Woolwich in south-east London and only moved to Highbury in 1913 — directly into Tottenham's patch. Spurs have never forgiven them. In 1919, Arsenal were controversially promoted to the First Division ahead of Spurs despite finishing only fifth in the Second Division. The grudge is over a century old.

keyMoments.text: Royal Arsenal founded in Woolwich, south-east London
Arsenal relocate to Highbury — directly into Tottenham's territory
Arsenal controversially promoted to First Division ahead of Spurs
Arsenal clinch the Double at White Hart Lane, beating Spurs 1-0
Arsenal go unbeaten for the entire season — the Invincibles
Arsenal beat Spurs 3-2 at the Tottenham Hotspur Stadium in a dramatic comeback

strapline: Just four miles apart in the Black Country. Walsall's finest hour was arguably beating Arsenal 2-1 in the 1933 FA Cup third round — still regarded as one of the greatest FA Cup upsets of all time.

The Manchester Derby. United were originally "Newton Heath LYR" (a railway workers' team) while City were "Ardwick AFC." When Newton Heath nearly went bankrupt in 1902, legend has it the club was saved by a brewery owner's dog — the club was renamed Manchester United. The rest is history, and City fans have never let United forget they weren't always the bigger club.

Gloucestershire neighbours, ten miles apart. Forest Green, under chairman Dale Vince, became the world's first vegan football club — no meat sold at the ground, organic pitch, solar-powered lawnmower. Cheltenham, more traditionally run, look across the county with bemusement.

Somerset rivals. Yeovil were the great giant-killers of non-league football in the post-war years — their sloping pitch at Huish Park became legendary. They finally reached the Football League in 2003 after decades of trying.

Across the Mersey. Tranmere can see the city of Liverpool from Prenton Park but exist in a completely different footballing universe. The clubs met in the FA Cup fourth round in 2001 — Liverpool won 4-2 at Prenton Park on the way to their cup treble.

Pennine neighbours in the hills east of Manchester. Both have spent most of their existence in the lower divisions. Oldham's Boundary Park is one of the highest and most exposed grounds in England — visiting players and fans learn to dress warm.

Separated by the Mersey and the Birkenhead tunnel. Tranmere's Prenton Park looks across the river to Liverpool's skyline. During their 2000s golden era under John Aldridge (a Liverpool legend), Tranmere occasionally drew bigger crowds than Everton for midweek games. That particularly stung.

The other Devon rivalry. Only 18 miles apart along the coast. Exeter once toured South America in 1914 and played against Brazil — making them the first non-South American team to play the Brazilian national side.

The Tees-Wear derby. Hartlepool are famously the club whose mascot, H'Angus the Monkey (named after a legend about locals hanging a shipwrecked monkey they mistook for a French spy during the Napoleonic Wars), was elected as the town's mayor in 2002. He served three terms.

The Class of 92 connection — Salford are co-owned by Gary Neville, Ryan Giggs, Paul Scholes, Nicky Butt, and David Beckham. The Peninsula Stadium is three miles from Old Trafford. United fans are divided: some see it as flattery, others as an annoying sideshow.

South London neighbours with a shared non-league heritage. Sutton are best known for their 1989 FA Cup giant-killing of Coventry City — the holders at the time. Goalkeeper Dave Beasant had won the Cup with Wimbledon the previous year; Sutton knocked his old team's Cup victim out the next season.

Greater Manchester rivals whose hatred intensified during their simultaneous Premier League years (2005-2012). Before Wigan's rise, Bolton fans barely acknowledged them. After Wigan won the FA Cup in 2013 (beating Manchester City) and were relegated the same week, even Bolton had to grudgingly respect them.

The Dockers Derby. Both clubs emerged from the Thames Ironworks and docklands of east and south-east London in the 1890s. The rivalry is among the most intense in English football and police regularly deploy thousands of officers for fixtures between them. The clubs haven't played each other in the league since 2012.

North Yorkshire neighbours. Harrogate are the new kids — they only reached the Football League in 2020 via the National League play-offs, during the COVID season. Their Wetherby Road ground (now EnviroVent Stadium) was upgraded hastily to meet EFL requirements.

The Tyne-Wear Derby. This rivalry predates organised football — the cities have been competing since medieval times. Sunderland's official nickname "The Black Cats" was only adopted in 2000 after a vote. The derby has produced some extraordinary moments, including Sunderland's 3-0 win at St James' Park in 1979 that some credit with costing Newcastle manager Bill McGarry his job.

This rivalry has cultural undertones — historically, Spurs drew support from the Jewish community in north London while West Ham's East End base had its own distinct identity. On the pitch, the fixture has produced some extraordinary moments, including West Ham's famous 1-0 win at White Hart Lane when Carlos Tevez kept them up on the final day of 2006/07.
South-east London/Kent borders. Welling's Park View Road is one of the most unassuming grounds in English football — a non-league outpost surrounded by suburban streets.

South London neighbours. Bromley were one of the great amateur clubs of the post-war era, winning the FA Amateur Cup multiple times. Their modern Hayes Lane ground sits just four miles from Selhurst Park.

Lancashire coast rivals, just seven miles apart. Blackpool's Bloomfield Road has seen FA Cup finals (1953, the "Matthews Final") and Premier League football (2010/11). Fleetwood, owned by fishing industry magnate Andy Pilley, rose from the Northern Premier League to League One in just eight years.

Kent rivals. The original Maidstone United reached the Football League in 1989 but went bust just three years later. The current club, reformed in 1992, have worked their way back up to the National League South. Gillingham, meanwhile, have survived in the Football League since 1920.

20 meetings since 1962. The 2025 League One play-off final at Wembley — Charlton 1-0 Orient, Gillesphey's free-kick, 76,193 in attendance — elevated this from a local fixture to a genuine rivalry. Orient are still processing it.
keyMoments: League One play-off final 2025 — Charlton 1-0 Orient at Wembley, Gillesphey free-kick sealed promotion to the Championship
Charlton won both league meetings in 2024-25 before beating Orient again in the play-off final
Orient won 1-0 at Brisbane Road on New Year's Eve 2011 in League One
Original Wimbledon and QPR were top-flight rivals throughout the late 1980s and 1990s
A proposed merger "Wimbledon Rangers" around 2000-01 was ultimately rejected
Wimbledon beat QPR 2-1 in FA Cup February 1997

strapline: Both from West Yorkshire but worlds apart in profile. Bradford's time in the Premier League (1999-2001) included a miraculous final-day survival in 2000, beating Liverpool 1-0 when they needed a win to stay up.

Lincolnshire rivals. Lincoln's 2016/17 FA Cup run — reaching the quarter-finals as a non-league club, the first to do so since 1914 — captivated the nation. Danny Cowley took them from the National League to League One.

The Christmas Eve 1983 thriller — Brentford 3, Wimbledon 4 — was the last Football League match ever played on a Christmas Eve. The old Wimbledon earned its reputation for chaos. The new one is still trying.
keyMoments: Christmas Eve 1983 — Brentford 3-4 Wimbledon, the last Football League match ever played on Christmas Eve
Both were Third Division rivals in 1983-84

strapline: The South Yorkshire derby, ~9 miles apart. Rotherham didn't win a league match at Oakwell for 56 years — from 1969 to 2025. That's 20,672 days. Oakwell has been called a graveyard for Millers managers, and the tunnel runs right past the away end. It's not a comfortable place to lose.
keyMoments: Rotherham's 56-year Oakwell hoodoo — no league win there from March 1969 to October 2025, finally broken by Kian Spence's half-volley
Barnsley 4-0 Rotherham in August 2016 — 4,000 Millers fans jeered manager Alan Stubbs at the tunnel; he was sacked eight games later
Mark Robins left as Rotherham manager to join Barnsley in September 2009 while Rotherham were top of League Two — seen as a betrayal
Barnsley 2-0 Rotherham in November 2024 — Rotherham chairman Tony Stewart spotted booing his own team from the directors' box before leaving early
Rotherham haven't done the league double over Barnsley since 1952/53 — over 70 years ago

strapline: South Yorkshire neighbours. Doncaster's Belle Vue was one of the great old English grounds before they moved to the Keepmoat. Rotherham's New York Stadium sits on the site of a former steelworks — the name comes from the old Guest and Chrimes factory.
Morecambe Bay rivals. You can see across the bay from one town to the other, but driving round takes over an hour. It's a properly remote corner of English football.

North Lincolnshire rivals, ~27 miles apart across the Humber flatlands. Grimsby's Blundell Park is actually in Cleethorpes, not Grimsby. Scunthorpe are famous for their unintentional run-in with email spam filters — their name contains an unfortunate substring.

Somerset vs Bristol, 12 miles apart. Bath City have been a non-league fixture since their founding in 1889. Their Twerton Park ground was famously used by Bristol Rovers as a temporary home from 1986-1996 when Rovers had no ground of their own.

South Devon rivals. Torquay were a Football League club from 1927 to 2014, when they were relegated to the Conference. Their Plainmoor ground is famous for its views of Torbay — arguably the most scenic ground in English football.

Arsenal's original local rivalry — back when they were in Plumstead, not Highbury. 28 meetings since 1893. The 1988 FA Cup tie saw 48 arrests and two pubs destroyed. In 1995 Millwall knocked them out at Highbury. Some fixtures carry a certain reputation.
keyMoments: 1988 FA Cup 3rd round — Arsenal won 2-0 but the match was overshadowed by severe hooliganism: 48 arrests, two pubs destroyed
1995 FA Cup 3rd round replay — Millwall stunned Arsenal 2-0 at Highbury, a famous giant-killing
1890s — as South London neighbours (Arsenal in Plumstead, Millwall on Isle of Dogs), this was Arsenal's original local rivalry

strapline: Both London clubs, but at opposite ends of the city. This rivalry was mostly dormant until Roman Abramovich's 2003 takeover turned Chelsea into a superpower, directly threatening Arsenal's position. The 2004 Champions League quarter-final (Wayne Bridge's winner) and multiple Cup finals fuelled the fire.

The cross-border derby. Wrexham play in Wales, Chester in England, twelve miles apart. The rivalry took on new life after Ryan Reynolds and Rob McElhenney bought Wrexham in 2020, turning a modest Welsh club into a global brand. Chester fans remain distinctly unimpressed.

Most people know Accrington Stanley from the 1980s milk advert ("Accrington Stanley? Who are they? Exactly!"). But the original Accrington FC were founding members of the Football League in 1888 — they resigned from the league in 1893. The current club are a completely separate entity, founded in 1968.

Edgeley Park is roughly four miles from the Etihad. When City were in their wilderness years (playing in the third tier in 1998/99), Stockport were actually above them in the second tier. City fans of a certain age remember that, even if the club's newer global fanbase doesn't.

keyMoments: Barnet lost 1-2 to QPR in a League Cup tie after joining the Football League in 1991
Meetings extremely rare given the gulf in divisions
Millwall have dominated — won 4, drawn 3, AFC Wimbledon never won
The original Wimbledon and Millwall were fellow south London top-flight clubs in the late 1980s
South London geography adds extra edge

strapline: 17 meetings since 1914, and Millwall actually lead the head-to-head. Chelsea have never beaten Millwall in the FA Cup — not once. In 1995, after two draws, Millwall won on penalties at Stamford Bridge. The fixture's reputation precedes it.
keyMoments: Chelsea have never beaten Millwall in the FA Cup — Millwall won in 1914 and 1937 (3-0 at The Den, 41,503 crowd)
1995 FA Cup 4th round — after 0-0 and 1-1 draws, Millwall won 5-4 on penalties; marred by significant post-match violence
Millwall actually lead the all-time head-to-head (6 wins to Chelsea's 5) despite the gulf in league status
Arsenal's record: 27 wins, 13 draws, 14 losses across 54 league meetings
QPR's famous 2-1 home win over Arsenal in March 2012 during their relegation fight
Arsenal 6-0 QPR in FA Cup 2001 — comprehensive demolition

strapline: Technically neighbours. Spiritually, different planets. Fulham are a Premier League club on the Thames; Sutton are a non-league side from the suburbs. They have never met in a competitive fixture. The only thing connecting them is a shared county boundary drawn by a bureaucrat.

Surrey-Hampshire border rivals, 12 miles apart. Woking's Tim Buzaglo scored a hat-trick against West Bromwich Albion in the 1991 FA Cup third round — one of non-league football's most famous individual performances.

The Cambridgeshire derby, ~30 miles apart. Peterborough are famous for their "Posh" nickname and for scoring goals in absurd quantities — they hit 134 in 1960/61, a Football League record for a single season.
keyMoments: First ever meeting in 1956 Hunts Premier Cup — Peterborough won 5-1
Cambridge won four consecutive league derbies in 1988-90 as both clubs earned promotion from Division Four
16-year gap (2001-2017) after Cambridge relegation — reunion preceded by smoke grenade incident at a pub
Cambridge 2-0 Peterborough in April 2023 — first league win over Posh in 25 years, denting their auto-promotion push
Back-to-back record thrashings: Peterborough 5-0 (Nov 2023) and 6-1 (Nov 2024) with Kwame Poku hat-trick
Bromley only joined the Football League in 2024 — very limited history
Charlton won 1-0 in the 2024-25 EFL Trophy — first competitive meeting
New south-east London derby with both clubs based in the SE postcode area
First met in Division Two in November 1900 — Stockport won 1-0 at Green Lane
1978 League Cup — Stockport led 2-1 at Old Trafford before McIlroy equalised in injury time and a controversial penalty won it 3-2
18 of 19 meetings came in Division Two in the early 1900s — Stockport won 7 of 9 home fixtures
Legendary 5-5 League Cup draw in 1991 — one of Barnet's first ever Football League fixtures
Brentford dominated: won 5, drew 2, Barnet never won
Both north/west London clubs who shared lower-division status in the 1990s-2000s

strapline: The South Coast Derby. Separated by ~16 miles of the M27. Portsmouth fans call Southampton "Scummers," Saints fans call Pompey "Skates." The rivalry intensified when Southampton were founding members of the Premier League in 1992 while Portsmouth languished in the lower divisions. Pompey's 2008 FA Cup win remains a sore point for Saints fans.

Nottinghamshire neighbours. Mansfield's Field Mill is one of the oldest grounds still in use — football has been played on the site since 1861, making it arguably the oldest football ground in the world.

keyMoments: Cheltenham 5-0 Swindon in FA Cup November 2014 — comprehensive thrashing
Swindon 3-3 Cheltenham in March 2025 — Swindon came back from two goals down in a six-goal thriller
Cheltenham dominant in the head-to-head with 10 wins from 20 meetings
Bromley only entered the Football League in 2024 — competitive history barely exists
Both south-east London clubs with local derby potential going forward
Arsenal won their League Cup tie comfortably
Barnet are the closest Football League club geographically to Arsenal's old Highbury ground
Gulf in divisions means competitive meetings limited to cup draws

strapline: The Black Country Derby. Just three miles separate Molineux and The Hawthorns. Both were founding members of the Football League in 1888. The A41 between them is one of the most tension-filled stretches of road in English football on derby day.

Another South Yorkshire pairing. Barnsley's Oakwell ground has hosted rugby league, cricket, and even baseball over the years. Wednesday's Hillsborough is forever associated with the 1989 disaster — the most solemn ground in English football.

keyMoments: Barrow 4-0 Fleetwood in August 2011 — Andy Cook hat-trick in Conference Premier
Fleetwood 4-1 Barrow in January 2012 — Jamie Vardy scored twice before his record transfer to Leicester
Fleetwood 3-2 Barrow in February 2026 — dramatic comeback from 2-0 down with winner in 90+4

strapline: 71 meetings since 1920. The record? 27 wins each, 17 draws. Perfectly balanced, as all things should be. The fixture vanished when Newport were expelled from the Football League in 1988 and only returned when they clawed their way back in 2013.
keyMoments: Remarkably even: 27 wins each and 17 draws across 71 matches — a perfectly balanced rivalry
Both founding members of the Third Division South in 1920
Fixture disappeared when Newport were expelled from the Football League in 1988, resumed when they returned in 2013

strapline: "The Devon Derby" — 36 miles of the A38 behind a caravan. Devon fans insist this is their local derby, and when your nearest alternative is in Cornwall, they've got a point. But 36 miles is 36 miles, and no amount of cream teas changes that.

keyMoments: Very evenly matched — approximately 8 wins each with 11-12 draws in modern records
QPR won 4-1 in one of the earliest meetings in 1937-38
A classic London derby with decades of history across multiple divisions

strapline: The A38 Derby, ~13 miles apart. Nigel Clough is the thread — managed Burton, left for Derby, came back to Burton and led them to the Championship. Burton beat Derby 1-0 on their Championship debut in 2016. Against his former club. Of course.
keyMoments: Burton beat Derby 1-0 on their Championship debut in August 2016 — managed by Nigel Clough against his former club
Nigel Clough is the central figure — managed Burton, left for Derby (2009-13), returned to Burton and led them to the Championship
Derby 3-2 Burton in January 2024 — Hourihane stoppage-time winner after Burton clawed back from 2-0 down
Barnet beat Watford 3-2 in one meeting — a notable scalp
The Hertfordshire derby — until Barnet joined the League, Watford were Hertfordshire's only League club
In 1974 Millwall hosted the first ever Sunday football match in England — against Fulham
Fulham dominated recent meetings: 6 wins, 3 draws, 2 losses since 2014
Fulham 4-0 Millwall in August 2019 was the most emphatic recent result
First competitive meeting in January 2018 — 2-2 draw in the National League, both still in non-league
League Two play-off semi-final 2023 — Stockport won on penalties after 2-2 on aggregate, Sarcevic with the decisive pen
Salford 3-1 Stockport in EFL Trophy October 2025 — Salford's most emphatic win in the fixture
South London derby gaining significance since Bromley entered the EFL in 2024
Evenly split — 2 wins each and a draw

strapline: West Yorkshire rivals. Bradford's Valley Parade fire in 1985 killed 56 people — the worst disaster at an English football ground until Hillsborough four years later. The tragedy changed stadium safety regulations across the country.

keyMoments: AFC Wimbledon historically had the edge, winning 4 of 7 recent meetings
Leyton Orient 4-2 AFC Wimbledon in March 2026 — their biggest win in the fixture
South/east London rivalry sharing League Two and League One
Original Wimbledon and Arsenal were regular top-flight opponents in the 1986-2000 era
Arsenal's league record vs old Wimbledon: W7 D4 L5 in the Premier League — closer than you'd think
AFC Wimbledon's only meeting: 0-3 Carabao Cup loss in September 2021
FA Cup 1994 — Barnet held Chelsea 0-0 at Stamford Bridge before losing the replay 4-0
Rarely in the same division — very limited competitive history
Barnet beat Spurs U23s 2-1 in the EFL Trophy at The Hive in front of 445 people
FA Cup 1953 — set Field Mill's all-time record attendance of 24,467 which still stands; Forest won 1-0
League Cup 1999-2000 — Forest won 3-1 on aggregate across two legs
Just 3 competitive meetings ever despite being 14 miles apart in Nottinghamshire
Charlton stunned Arsenal 4-2 at Highbury in November 2001 during Arsenal's Double-winning season
Arsenal dominated Premier League era — won 11 of 15 from 1998 onward

strapline: Twelve miles apart in the Peak District foothills. Chesterfield's famous crooked spire church overshadows the ground. In the 1997 FA Cup semi-final, Chesterfield were denied a blatantly good goal against Middlesbrough when 2-1 up — they would have reached the final. The "ghost goal" still haunts Spireites fans.

keyMoments: Orient's famous 1972 FA Cup 5th round comeback — 2-0 down, won 3-2 at Brisbane Road
First met in 1905-06 — Chelsea won 3-0 then 6-1 against Clapton Orient
First met in 1901-02 when Doncaster entered Division Two alongside Barnsley
25-year gap (1979-2004) after Barnsley's promotion prevented the rivalry from truly developing
Doncaster 1-0 Barnsley in March 2026 — ex-Barnsley player Elliot Lee scored the penalty winner

strapline: South coast neighbours across the Dorset-Devon border — pleasant scenery, questionable football. Torquay's Plainmoor ground offers views of the English Riviera, which is fortunate because the football hasn't always been worth watching. Both clubs have bounced around the non-league system like a ball on a shingle beach.

keyMoments: 1996 First Division play-off semi-final — Palace knocked Charlton out 3-1 on aggregate
May 2005 Premier League relegation day — Charlton 2-2 Palace, the draw confirmed Palace's relegation
Palace won 13 of the first 20 meetings and completed the league double six times between 1926 and 2012
Both founding members of the Football League in 1888 — Derby won both meetings that first season (3-2 and 5-3)
1894-95 Test Match — Derby beat Notts County 2-1 to retain First Division status in the precursor to modern play-offs
Derby 3-0 Notts County in March 1953 — the first ever match under floodlights at the Baseball Ground
January 2025 — Leeds beat Harrogate 1-0 at Elland Road in FA Cup 3rd round, 69 league places apart
Harrogate only entered the Football League in 2020
The clubs are ~17 miles apart in North/West Yorkshire
Both in the Third Division South from the 1920s — shared many league seasons
Cardiff dominates the overall record with significantly more wins
Last league meeting was 1999-2000 in the Second Division

strapline: 120 meetings since 1896. The earliest recorded encounter produced an 11-goal thriller. The most recent, a 2017 FA Cup quarter-final, saw Son score a hat-trick in a 6-0 win overshadowed by racist chanting. A fixture with a long and complicated history.
keyMoments: September 1896 — earliest recorded meeting produced a remarkable 5-6 thriller, 11 goals
February 1967 FA Cup replay — 58,189 attendance, largest crowd for a Millwall home match against Spurs
March 2017 FA Cup QF — Son Heung-min hat-trick in 6-0 win, overshadowed by racist chanting from Millwall fans
Remarkably even: 5 wins each with 3 draws across 13 matches
Stevenage only entered the Football League in 2010; earlier meetings were in non-league
~15 miles apart in Hertfordshire/Bedfordshire, direct league rivals in recent seasons

strapline: 44 meetings since 1910. The 1990 FA Cup semi-final is the one — Oldham, from the Second Division, drew 3-3 with Man Utd at Maine Road in one of the great cup ties. Robins won the replay. Oldham have been dining out on those 90 minutes ever since.
keyMoments: 1990 FA Cup semi-final — epic 3-3 draw at Maine Road, Second Division Oldham nearly pulled off huge upset before Robins won the replay 2-1
Boxing Day 1991 — Man Utd won 6-3 away at Oldham in a wild Division One encounter
Oldham spent 3 seasons in the top flight (1991-94) providing regular meetings
Long gap — hadn't met competitively between 1982 and 2021
August 2021 EFL Cup — QPR won 5-3 on penalties after 1-1 draw

strapline: South Yorkshire rivals, ten miles apart. Barnsley are one of the Premier League's shortest-staying members — they survived one season in 1997/98. Their fans still talk about that year the way other clubs talk about winning trophies.

Twenty miles of the Lancashire coast between them. Morecambe spent decades in non-league before reaching League Two in 2007 with a ground smaller than most Premier League car parks. Blackpool, meanwhile, had a whole Premier League season to their name. Neither is really in a position to brag.

keyMoments: Port Vale leads the overall record: 9 wins vs 6, with 8 draws
Frequent opponents in League One and League Two in recent decades
A Staffordshire-Shropshire border rivalry
A traditional Greater Manchester derby stretching back to pre-WW1
Stockport's record attendance was set vs Oldham on 21 December 1907
Last 7 meetings evenly split — 3 wins each and 1 draw
Orient dominated with 4 wins to Barnet's 2 and 3 draws
Orient won 10-9 on aggregate across 9 meetings — perfectly tight
Both in the Third Division South from the 1920s
FA Cup 1936-37 — Newport beat Bristol City 3-0 at home
Newport out of the Football League entirely 1988-2013, creating a long gap
Bristol Rovers dominate: 13 wins to Cheltenham's 9 with 7 draws
60% win rate and outscored Cheltenham 42-28 overall
Regular opponents since Cheltenham rose to the Football League in 1999

strapline: Both founding members of the Football League in 1888. Preston went unbeaten as The Invincibles that first season — the original ones, long before Arsenal's. 89 meetings, ~10 miles apart, and Preston still lead the all-time record.
keyMoments: Both founding members of the Football League in 1888 — Preston went unbeaten as The Invincibles that first season
November 1967 — 8-goal thriller at Deepdale, Blackburn winning 5-3
Reunited in the Championship post-2015 after years apart, reigniting the Lancashire rivalry
Very evenly matched: AFC Wimbledon 6 wins, Charlton 5, 5 draws
A south London derby only possible since AFC Wimbledon climbed into the Football League in 2011
AFC Wimbledon have had the edge in recent meetings
January 2019 FA Cup 4th round — AFC Wimbledon 4-2 West Ham, one of the great FA Cup upsets
League One's bottom club beat Premier League West Ham at Kingsmeadow (4,777 attendance)
Bookmakers had Wimbledon at 500/1

strapline: The West Midlands Derby. Wolves and Villa are the two most successful clubs in the region. Villa won the European Cup in 1982; Wolves won the league three times in the 1950s. The rivalry is intensified by both clubs' belief that they, not the other, are the true big club of the West Midlands.

Cheshire vs Staffordshire, ~13 miles apart. Crewe's Dario Gradi era (1983-2007) produced an extraordinary production line of talent: Dean Ashton, Danny Murphy, Seth Johnson, Rob Jones, David Platt, and more all came through their youth system.

Cornwall's only senior club vs Devon's biggest — ~41 miles of the A38 and a county border. Truro are the most south-westerly club in the English football system, which sounds romantic until you realise it mainly means nobody visits.

keyMoments: AFC Wimbledon won both League Two meetings — only shared one season
Orient gave Premier League Fulham a scare in the 2005-06 FA Cup
Fulham came from behind to win 3-2 in the 2016 League Cup at Brisbane Road
Bolton have won all 5 competitive meetings, scoring 13 and conceding just 2
Bolton 5-1 Salford in August 2022 — the most emphatic result
A new Greater Manchester derby born from Salford's rise to the Football League in 2019
MK Dons dominate overall: 11 wins to Northampton's 5 with 4 draws
Northampton have had the upper hand recently — 4 wins in last 5 league meetings
Fierce local derby between two towns ~20 miles apart

strapline: Twenty-nine miles of the A420 — barely a rivalry, more of a long commute. Swindon reached the Premier League in 1993 and got immediately relegated. Their 1969 League Cup win over Arsenal remains sacred locally, which tells you everything about the size of the club. Both insist this is a derby. The map disagrees.

keyMoments: Charlton's record: 11 wins, 27 draws, 38 losses — Spurs heavily dominant
May 2007 — Spurs sealed Charlton's relegation from the Premier League
Both shared many seasons in the First Division/Premier League, especially the 2000s

strapline: Both London, but eight miles and a cultural gulf apart. The Battle of the Bridge in 2016 (nine yellows, Leicester won the league instead) was great theatre, but this is less a derby and more a recurring scheduling headache. Spurs fans despise Chelsea; Chelsea fans aren't sure they think about Spurs at all.
East Midlands neighbours straddling the Nottinghamshire-Derbyshire border. Both are proper market town clubs — the kind that have been the backbone of the lower leagues for over a century.

The East Midlands Derby. Brian Clough managed Derby to the First Division title in 1972, then resigned in a dispute with the chairman. He joined Brighton briefly, then took over at arch-rivals Forest — and won the European Cup twice. Derby fans worshipped him, then watched him become a god at the club down the A52.

keyMoments: Harrogate dominated early — won 7 of 12 meetings
Yorkshire derby since Harrogate entered the Football League in 2020
Southampton 7-0 Bournemouth in October 1957 — Southampton's largest victory in the fixture
Bournemouth's 2015 Premier League promotion transformed this from a lower-league encounter into a marquee local derby
Bournemouth won both 2024-25 Premier League meetings 3-1, climbing to fifth in the table
First met in 1964-65 League Cup — Stoke won the replay 1-0
Shrewsbury won 5 of 12 league meetings — better record than you'd expect against a top-flight club
FA Cup 2019 — Shrewsbury came from 2-0 down to win 3-2 at the bet365 Stadium, a famous giant-killing

strapline: The M4 Derby, ~25 miles apart. 147 meetings since 1891 — Swindon lead 64-61, which tells you everything about how tight this is. Both promoted together, both relegated together. The West Country's most enduring football rivalry.
keyMoments: Known as the M4 Derby or West Country Derby — 147 meetings since 1891
Swindon leads all-time 64-61 with 22 draws — extremely tight
Both promoted and relegated together 2007-2011

strapline: 100 meetings. West London vs East London, a century of it. West Ham used the 1975 FA Cup as a stepping stone to lifting the trophy. QPR hold a grudge. Everyone in W12 and E13 has an opinion.
keyMoments: 1975 FA Cup — West Ham beat QPR 2-1 in the 5th round en route to winning the Cup
Classic West London vs East London derby spanning over a century
West Ham holds an advantage in the all-time record
February 1897 — Bromley beat Thames Iron Works (West Ham's predecessor) 2-0 in the London Senior Cup
No competitive meeting in over 125 years
Bromley only entered the Football League in 2024 — future cup meetings now possible
Rotherham dominates the recent record: 11 wins to Chesterfield's 6
A cross-border Derbyshire/South Yorkshire rivalry, ~12 miles apart
Both clubs have fluctuated between League One and League Two creating frequent meetings

strapline: Cumbria's best attempt at a derby — 55 miles apart across some of England's emptiest countryside. Getting to either ground requires genuine commitment and a full tank. When Barrow returned to League Two in 2020 after 48 years, Cumbria had two professional clubs again for the first time since the 70s. That's still not a derby though.

70 meetings since 1907. The 1982 FA Cup Final — QPR's only final appearance — ended 1-1 before Spurs won the replay 1-0 with a Hoddle penalty. QPR fans still talk about what might have been. Spurs fans barely remember it happened.
keyMoments: 1982 FA Cup Final — 1-1 draw then Spurs won replay 1-0 with Hoddle penalty; QPR's only FA Cup Final
Spurs dominated the Premier League era meetings
Charlton beat Chelsea 4-2 at The Valley on Boxing Day 2003 in the Premier League
Charlton won on penalties in the 2005 League Cup after 1-1 draw
Chelsea 5-1 Charlton in FA Cup January 2026 — most recent meeting
Both shared League One in 2011-12 — Wednesday won 3-1, Chesterfield won 1-0
Wednesday lead the modern h2h 5-2 with 1 draw

strapline: 66 meetings since 1921. Orient beat Palace 8-0 in Division Three South in November 1955 — still one of the most lopsided results in either club's history. Palace lead the all-time record 29-18, but that scoreline will never be forgotten.
keyMoments: Orient beat Palace 8-0 in Division 3 South in November 1955 — a record thrashing
Palace lead all-time 29-18 with 19 draws across 66 meetings

strapline: 141 meetings since 1894, ~14 miles apart across the West Midlands. Wolves lead the all-time record convincingly at 67-41. Not quite the Black Country derby, not quite the Second City derby — but 130 years of history says this one matters.
keyMoments: First met in the 1894-95 season — one of the oldest fixtures in English football
141 meetings with Wolves dominant: 67 wins to Birmingham's 41
Birmingham 3-2 Wolves in April 2007 — one of the most memorable recent clashes in the Championship

strapline: The South Wales Derby. The only two Welsh clubs in the English league system, and they absolutely despise each other. Cardiff call Swansea "Jacks", Swansea call Cardiff "Bluebirds" (or worse). 119 meetings since 1912, and the intensity has only grown as both clubs have risen through the leagues. Geography says 34 miles. The hatred says next door.

100 meetings since 1920. QPR hold a slight all-time edge at 38 wins to Palace's 32, but 30 draws tell you how evenly matched this has been. A London rivalry that's been simmering for over a century without ever quite boiling over.
keyMoments: Longstanding London rivalry with 100 meetings since 1920
QPR hold a slight all-time edge at 38 wins vs Palace's 32 with 30 draws
EFL Trophy September 2024 — Chelsea U21s beat Bromley 3-2; no senior meeting

strapline: 40 meetings since 1920. In 1965, someone threw a hand grenade onto the pitch at Griffin Park during a Brentford–Millwall match. Goalkeeper Chic Brodie picked it up. It was a dummy. This fixture has always had a certain energy.
keyMoments: 1965 — a hand grenade was thrown onto the pitch at Griffin Park during Brentford vs Millwall; keeper Chic Brodie picked it up (it was a dummy)
Millwall wore a camouflage kit against Brentford in November 2014 for the WWI centenary
Minimal competitive history — gulf in divisions throughout their histories
Newport beat Cheltenham in the 1937 Welsh Cup quarter-final
Modern rivalry resumed in 2013 — 13+ league meetings since, very evenly matched at 6 wins each
February 2009 — Doncaster beat Wednesday 1-0, their first ever league victory over the Owls
September 2013 — Doncaster won 1-0 away at Hillsborough in the Championship, another notable upset
Sheffield Wednesday won both 2021-22 League One fixtures while Doncaster were relegated
Lincoln hammered Peterborough 5-2 in FA Cup January 2026
Peterborough lead the all-time record 23-15 with 18 draws

strapline: West Yorkshire neighbours who'd love to call this a derby. Huddersfield won the league three times in a row (1924-26) — back when Leeds were nothing. A century later the roles have thoroughly reversed, and Huddersfield's claim to rivalry is mostly nostalgic.

West Midlands neighbours. Coventry's greatest moment — winning the 1987 FA Cup final against Tottenham with Keith Houchen's diving header — remains one of the most iconic goals in Cup history. It's replayed so often that even Birmingham fans know every frame.

keyMoments: 1891-92 — Doncaster beat Sheffield United 2-1 at Bramall Lane to win the Sheffield & Hallamshire Cup final
2008-09 Championship — Billy Sharp, on loan from Sheff Utd, scored 15 goals for Doncaster including in the derby
New Year's Day 2013 — 2-2 draw at the Keepmoat in League One
Rarely in the same division — limited competitive history
Hull beat Lincoln 3-0 in October 2003; Lincoln won 2-0 in February 2004
Never met competitively — Bromley only entered the Football League in 2024
Barnet beat Millwall 2-0 in the FL Trophy in 2009-10
Perfectly balanced at 5 wins each with 4 draws
Brentford 3-1 Leyton Orient in League Cup September 2024 — most recent meeting
FA Cup 3rd round January 2018 at Wembley — Spurs won 3-0 with Kane scoring twice
AFC Wimbledon's Jimmy Abdou hit the crossbar and held Spurs scoreless for 63 minutes

strapline: Both founding members of the Football League in 1888. 170 meetings and counting — Bolton have beaten Blackburn more times than any other club in their history. The 2012 meeting, Bolton's first match after Fabrice Muamba's cardiac arrest, transcended football.
keyMoments: Both founding members of the Football League in 1888 — Bolton have more league wins vs Blackburn than vs any other club
August 1995 — Bolton's first ever Premier League match, beat reigning champions Blackburn 2-1 with late Stubbs header
March 2012 — Bolton's first match after Fabrice Muamba's cardiac arrest, emotionally charged 2-1 win over Blackburn
Notts County 4-2 Leicester in FA Cup 1953 — a classic cup giant-killing at Filbert Street
Notts County 4-0 Leicester in August 1983 — a memorable away demolition, reversed months later when Leicester won 5-2
Last competitive meeting in 1993-94 — Notts County won 4-1; clubs have been in different divisions since

strapline: "The Thames Valley Derby" — a name that screams geography homework rather than footballing hatred. Twenty-five miles of commuter belt separate them. Oxford won the League Cup in 1986; Reading got 106 Championship points in 2005/06. Both memorable, neither enough to make this a proper derby.

121 meetings since 1892. The 1958 FA Cup Final — Bolton 2-0 Man Utd, three months after Munich — is the emotional centrepiece. Lofthouse bundled Gregg over the line for the second goal. He later admitted it was a foul.
keyMoments: 1958 FA Cup Final — Bolton beat Man Utd 2-0 just three months after Munich, Lofthouse brace with controversial bundled goal
Man Utd 7-2 Bolton in January 1958 — one of the last matches of the Busby Babes era, days before the Munich disaster
Premier League era — Man Utd dominated with 18 wins from 26 meetings, Bolton managed only 3 PL wins
August 2017 — Accrington stunned Championship Preston 3-2 in EFL Cup, Billy Kee 90+3' winner
November 2023 — Accrington beat Preston 3-2 again in Lancashire Senior Cup
Reformed Accrington (1968) has very limited history vs PNE; the original club would have had far more
2006 League One play-off semi-final — Barnsley overturned 1-0 deficit to win 3-2 on aggregate, went on to win the final
Huddersfield 2-1 Barnsley in April 2022 — sealed Barnsley's relegation from the Championship
Frequent modern clashes as both clubs yo-yo between Championship and League One
Only 3 competitive meetings ever despite being ~25 miles apart — never shared a league division
League Cup 2002 — first ever meeting, Derby won 3-1 at Field Mill
EFL Trophy 2022 — Mansfield won 6-5 on penalties after 1-1 draw, their only competitive result against Derby
Salford beat Wigan 2-0 in EFL Trophy September 2025
Salford only entered the Football League in 2019 — very short competitive history
Greater Manchester neighbours

strapline: The Tees-Wear derby, ~30 miles apart. 155 meetings stretching back to 1887 — older than the Tyne-Wear derby. The fixture that gave football its first four-figure transfer fee when Alf Common crossed the divide for £1,000 in 1905.
keyMoments: 1905 — Alf Common moved from Sunderland to Boro for £1,000, the first four-figure transfer in football history
Middlesbrough 6-0 Sunderland in 1936 — a demolition in the same season Sunderland won the league title
2012 FA Cup replay — Boro beat Sunderland 2-1 after extra time; Sunderland then went a decade without beating Boro
First met in 1978-79 Division Two — Stoke won 1-0 at the Racecourse in front of 20,211
FA Cup 2015 — Wrexham led in the 73rd minute before Arnautovic equalised and Ireland scored twice in stoppage time for 3-1
Stoke have won 11 of 12 league meetings — 92% win rate, the most one-sided fixture either club has

strapline: "The East Lancashire Derby" — though calling it that is generous given the 10-mile gap and a large hill in the way. These cotton mill towns have been suspicious of each other since the Industrial Revolution. Blackburn spent Jack Walker's millions on a Premier League title in 1995; Burnley spent theirs on... well, Burnley things. The hatred is real, even if the geography is a stretch.

"The Old Farm Derby" — at least the name is self-aware. Tractors instead of tenements, and a solid 40+ miles of Suffolk and Norfolk countryside between them. They genuinely despise each other, having played more than 100 times over the past 125 years, but geographically? Two counties and a lot of sugar beet fields say this one's a stretch driven by a lack of any meaningful opponents in their respective provinces.

keyMoments: Accrington smashed Bolton 7-1 in November 2019 after Bolton went down to 10 men
Bolton lead the h2h 5-4 with 1 draw

strapline: The other East Midlands derby, ~28 miles apart. 111 meetings, dead level at 42-41 in wins. Forest's 12-0 thrashing in 1909 remains Leicester's worst ever defeat — several players had been at a wedding the night before. Before Clough, this was THE East Midlands rivalry.
keyMoments: Forest 12-0 Leicester Fosse in April 1909 — Leicester's worst ever defeat, several players hungover from a wedding
Sustained top-flight rivalry through the 1950s-60s with both in Division One for a decade
Pre-Clough this was THE East Midlands derby — the two largest cities in the region, extremely closely matched (42-41 in wins)

strapline: 70 meetings since 1907. The September 1984 League Cup match ended 4-4 after extra time — Bolton's highest-scoring League Cup draw ever. A proper Lancashire derby between two clubs who've spent a century yo-yoing through the same divisions.
keyMoments: September 1984 — legendary League Cup match ended 4-4 after extra time
1994 FA Cup quarter-final — Oldham knocked Bolton out 1-0 at Burnden Park
Classic Lancashire derby with deep historical roots stretching back to pre-WW1

strapline: Sussex's attempt at a local rivalry. Twenty-five miles apart and about a hundred divisions of quality between them. Crawley's greatest day was beating Leeds 2-1 in the 2011 FA Cup as a non-league club. Brighton are now in the Premier League spending nine figures on transfers. Not exactly a meeting of equals.

Lancashire's "other" rivalry — 16 miles of the M55 and a lot of mutual side-eye. Preston were the first-ever league champions and the original "Invincibles." Blackpool had Stanley Matthews and the 1953 FA Cup Final. Both cling to their history because the present hasn't been as kind.

keyMoments: First met in 1936-37 when Villa were humiliatingly relegated to Division Two — Coventry beat them and finished above them
1987: Coventry won the FA Cup (their only major trophy) while Villa were relegated — brief role reversal
Dion Dublin transfer from Coventry to Villa in 1984 for £5.75m — scored 7 in his first 4 Villa games
Villa 3-2 Coventry, May 2001 — Coventry led 2-0 but collapsed, Merson 86th-min winner sealed relegation ending 34 years in the top flight
Have not met in any competition since 2001 — a 25-year gap and counting

strapline: Essex's version of a rivalry. Colchester once beat Don Revie's Leeds 3-2 in the 1971 FA Cup — one of the great giant-killings. Chelmsford are still waiting for their equivalent moment. At 20-odd miles apart, this is a regional grumble at best.

keyMoments: 1965-66 Division One — the only season both clubs shared the top flight, Northampton's famous single year
Leicester 4-0 Northampton in FA Cup 1998 — Marshall, Parker, Savage, and Cottee all scored
2008-09 League One — Leicester won 2-1 at Sixfields during their title-winning season climbing back from the third tier

strapline: Twenty-two miles apart, and Burton would love this to be considered a derby. They were a non-league club until 2009, so their brief Championship coexistence with Forest in 2016/17 was less a rivalry and more a once-in-a-lifetime holiday. Forest have the East Midlands Derby; Burton have wishful thinking.

124 meetings since 1900. The 1904 FA Cup Final — the first all-Lancashire final — saw Man City win 1-0 for their first ever major trophy. A hundred and twenty years later, the gap between these two clubs could hardly be wider.
keyMoments: 1904 FA Cup Final — first all-Lancashire final, Man City won 1-0 for their first major trophy
Bolton's record PL away defeat: 2-6 at Man City in October 2003
WBA 7-1 Coventry in October 1978 — Cunningham and Regis both scored twice in the era of the famous Three Degrees
Cyrille Regis transferred from WBA to Coventry in 1984 — went on to win the 1987 FA Cup with the Sky Blues
WBA 5-0 Coventry in December 2006 — Kamara scored after just 30 seconds
Coventry 3-2 WBA in November 2025 — came from 2-0 down against 10-man WBA to extend 8-point lead at top of Championship
Nearly two decades as Division One neighbours (1967-1986) cemented the fixture
Have never met in a competitive match despite being ~25 miles apart
All 4 recorded encounters are pre-season friendlies (2014, 2016, 2020, 2023)
Burton 3-0 Stoke in a 2016 friendly was the most eyebrow-raising result
Huddersfield hold the stronger record with 9 wins to Oldham's 6
Dominated by League One meetings in the 2000s
Both Lancashire clubs seesawing between League One and League Two
Accrington won 1-0 in a tight League Two clash in 2022
Both in the Premier League together 2005-2012 — intense Lancashire derbies
Wigan beat Blackburn 3-2 in January 2022 Championship thriller
Man Utd won 19 of 20 meetings, scoring 62 goals and conceding just 5
April 2012 — Wigan's sole victory, Maloney's 1-0 winner damaged Man Utd's title challenge
Three separate 5-0 thrashings — including Berbatov hat-trick in December 2011
New fixture born from Salford's rise to the Football League
Tight, low-scoring affairs typical of League Two derbies
Stockport surprisingly dominate the recent record: 5 wins to Bolton's 2 with 6 draws
Long gap of ~20 years without competitive meetings before reuniting in League One
Greater Manchester clubs ~10 miles apart

strapline: The East Midlands derby, ~15 miles apart. Derby fans still haven't forgiven the 1994 play-off final at Wembley. Leicester haven't forgiven the 12-0 in 1909 — though in fairness, half their team were hungover from a wedding.
keyMoments: First met in February 1894 — 0-0 draw at Filbert Street
1994 play-off final — Leicester beat Derby 2-1 at Wembley to win promotion, a lasting source of bitterness for Derby fans
Both clubs have inflicted 12-0 and 6-0 thrashings on each other — the 12-0 in 1909 came after Leicester players attended a wedding

strapline: 106 meetings since 1885. Preston beat Bolton 9-1 in the FA Cup in 1887 — still Bolton's record cup defeat. Bolton got their revenge in 2001, beating Preston 3-0 in the Division One play-off final at the Millennium Stadium to win promotion to the Premiership.
keyMoments: Preston beat Bolton 9-1 in FA Cup December 1887 — Bolton's record cup away defeat
Bolton beat Preston 3-0 in the 2001 Division One play-off final to win promotion to the Premiership
Walsall 3-0 Coventry in League Cup 1984 second leg — 4-2 on aggregate, giant-killing of top-flight Coventry
Coventry 6-1 Walsall in January 2004 — Coventry's biggest win in the fixture
Walsall 4-0 Coventry in April 2013 — Walsall's biggest win in a League One hammering

strapline: Nine meetings, ~25 miles apart. The 1985 FA Cup tie is one of football's strangest stories — Lineker scored a hat-trick in a 6-1 win, but after a piece of wood hit Burton's keeper, the FA ordered a replay behind closed doors. Leicester won 1-0. Lineker's hat-trick was expunged from the records.
keyMoments: FA Cup 1985 — Leicester won 6-1 (Lineker hat-trick) but FA ordered replay after wood thrown at Burton keeper; replay behind closed doors, Leicester won 1-0
Lineker's hat-trick was expunged from the records — one of football's strangest rulings
Burton's manager at the time of the 1985 match: a young Neil Warnock
Liverpool dominated — won 10 of 17 Premier League meetings
Wigan's famous 2013 FA Cup win was against Man City, not Liverpool
Lancashire derby created by Fleetwood's rapid rise from non-league
Preston won the majority of meetings

strapline: "The Bedfordshire-Hertfordshire derby" — a name only a county council could love. They're ~16 miles apart up the M1, which sounds close until you remember neither town is exactly a footballing metropolis. Luton banned away fans in the 1980s and installed a plastic pitch; Watford had Elton John. Both were weird in their own way.

keyMoments: Derby 7-1 Chesterfield on Easter Monday 1957 — during Derby's Third Division North title-winning season
FA Cup 2015 — first competitive meeting in 29 years, Derby won 2-0 with Bent and Hughes goals
Carabao Cup 2024 — Chesterfield led before Derby came back to win 2-1 at Pride Park

strapline: Buckinghamshire's attempt at a rivalry. When MK Dons parachuted into Milton Keynes, Wycombe suddenly had a neighbour — like someone building a Tesco Express next to your village shop. Adams Park sits in the Chiltern Hills looking down, both literally and metaphorically.

keyMoments: First met in February 1920 in Division Two — Coventry won 1-0 in their first Football League season
Coventry 3-1 Wolves, April 1967 — the Midlands Match of the Century, 51,455 attendance (Coventry record), both promoted
FA Cup 1984 third round needed three matches to settle — two 1-1 draws before Coventry won the second replay 3-0
Wolves 2-3 Coventry, March 2024 FA Cup QF — two goals in stoppage time for one of the great FA Cup comebacks
Reports of fighting between fan groups in the 1960s-70s when both were in the top flight together
1927-28 — Coventry fans rioted at Northampton after a dubious goal in the final game of a disastrous season
2013-14 — Coventry forced to play home games at Northampton's Sixfields for 13 months due to rent dispute; fans boycotted from nearby Jimmy's Hill
Northampton 2-1 Coventry in FA Cup November 2015 — upset at the Ricoh Arena

strapline: The biggest club rivalry in English football — but it's built on the cities' economic competition during the Industrial Revolution, not proximity. Manchester had the canals and railways; Liverpool had the port. In football terms, the two clubs have won a combined 39 league titles. But at 30 miles apart, this is a rivalry, not a derby.

Both founding members of the Football League in 1888. 145 meetings across three centuries. Derby hammered Stoke 4-0 during Clough's championship season. Stoke returned the favour by virtually sealing Derby's first ever relegation to the Third Division in 1955.
keyMoments: Both founding members of the Football League in 1888 — first league match ended 1-1
Derby hammered Stoke 4-0 during Clough's 1971-72 championship-winning season
Stoke 1-0 Derby in 1954-55 virtually sealed Derby's first-ever relegation to the Third Division
First league meetings in 1921-22 Division Two — both clubs in the lower leagues in the early days
Coventry 1-1 Derby, August 1969 — first top-flight meeting, Brian Clough's first season as a Division One manager
Coventry 6-2 Derby, April 2005 — the final ever match at Highfield Road, Coventry's home since 1899
Coventry 6-1 Derby, January 2006 — biggest ever win in the fixture, at the new Ricoh Arena
Derby 3-5 Coventry, August 2025 — eight-goal thriller, Coventry's first win at Derby in 11 visits

strapline: "The Roses Derby" — except it isn't one, is it? It traces back to the Wars of the Roses in the 15th century, which is a great pub argument but doesn't change the fact that they're nearly 40 miles apart. The football hatred peaked under Don Revie's Leeds in the late 60s/70s. Fierce? Absolutely. A derby? Have a word.

"The M23 Derby" 🙄. This one is ~37 miles of questionable hatred. It kicked off in the 1976/77 FA Cup when Palace knocked Brighton out, and fans clashed on the terraces. The violence escalated through the late 70s and 80s. Both clubs will tell you the other started it. The geographical distance is largely irrelevant, they care so much because they're both historically so irrelevant.

keyMoments: First met in April 1968 — Coventry's debut top-flight season, won 2-0 at Highfield Road
FA Cup fifth round 1987 — Coventry won 1-0 at the Victoria Ground, a pivotal step in their famous Cup-winning run
Stoke 4-0 Coventry in April 2023 — heaviest defeat in the fixture's history
FA Cup 1972-73 — Oxford (Division Three) stunned top-flight Coventry 4-1, putting them on the national map
FA Cup 1986-87 fifth round — Oxford won 3-2 in a replay despite Coventry's late fightback; Coventry still won the Cup that year
Coventry 6-2 Oxford in FA Cup January 2024 — three goals in 17 minutes in a demolition

strapline: Not a derby, but the site of one of the most infamous matches in English football history. "The Battle of Bramall Lane" in 2002 saw 3 red cards, multiple injuries, and an unprecedented abandonment when Sheffield United were reduced to 6 players. Not a rivalry — just a very bad day at the office.

keyMoments: Final day 1967-68 — Coventry's first top-flight survival by one point as Sheffield United were relegated
FA Cup QF 1997-98 — two 1-1 draws then Sheffield United won on penalties at Bramall Lane
Sheffield United 1-2 Coventry in February 2026 — Wright and Rudoni goals extended Coventry's lead at the top
FA Cup first round 1911 — Coventry beat The Wednesday 2-1 away before even joining the Football League
FA Cup QF 1987 — Coventry won 3-1 at Hillsborough en route to their only FA Cup triumph
Sheffield Wednesday 0-5 Coventry in October 2025 — match halted by pitch invasion, sent Coventry top of the Championship

strapline: Not a derby by any stretch, but the site of one of football's rarest moments. In 2019, Leeds scored while Villa's Jonathan Kodjia lay injured, sparking a mass confrontation. Leeds manager Marcelo Bielsa then instructed his players to let Villa walk the ball into the net unopposed. A managerial moral override — unprecedented, unrepeatable, and utterly Bielsa.

Wenger vs Ferguson was box office, yes. The pizza in the tunnel, the "Invincibles" — great telly. But calling this a derby is like calling a phone call a conversation over the garden fence. Two hundred miles apart. Come on.

Not a derby. Not even close geographically. But this is English football's most emotionally charged rivalry. When Wimbledon FC were controversially relocated to Milton Keynes in 2003 and rebranded as MK Dons, the original fans formed AFC Wimbledon and started from scratch in the 9th tier. Every meeting is about identity, heritage, and what it means to be a football club. The Franchise. Never forget, never forgive.
